era_context: The late 10th century CE continues with the same major powers and dynamics as 984. The Byzantine Empire under Basil II is in the midst of its Balkan campaigns. The Fatimid Caliphate remains a major Mediterranean power. In Western Europe, the Holy Roman Empire is fragmented; Otto III (r. 983–1002) has just become emperor as a child, creating a regency crisis. The Song Dynasty in China is consolidating its control. Scandinavian expansion and Viking settlement continue to reshape the North Atlantic and Eastern Europe.

events.title: Death of Otto II and Accession of Otto III (Holy Roman Empire)
events.description: Otto II, Holy Roman Emperor (r. 973–983), dies on December 7, 983, at approximately 28 years old. His death creates a succession crisis, as his son Otto III is only three years old. Otto III's accession as emperor is contested and requires the support of powerful German princes and the papacy. The regency period that follows is marked by internal conflict within the Holy Roman Empire, with various factions competing for influence over the young emperor. This succession crisis weakens the Holy Roman Empire's ability to project power and contributes to the fragmentation of Western European politics in the late 10th century.
key_figures: Otto III (Holy Roman Emperor, r. 983–1002, child)

sources.name: Thietmar of Merseburg, Chronicle (early 11th-century German chronicle)
Hrosvitha of Gandersheim, Gesta Ottonis (10th-century Latin poem)

events.certainty_note: Otto II's death on December 7, 983, is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. The date is certain.
cross_references: 1002-001 (Otto III's death and succession of Henry II)

events.title: Theophanu's Regency for Otto III
events.description: Following Otto II's death, his widow Theophanu (c. 955–991), a Byzantine princess, assumes the regency for the young Otto III. Theophanu is one of the most powerful women in 10th-century Europe, wielding significant political authority and attempting to stabilize the Holy Roman Empire during the succession crisis. Her regency is marked by efforts to maintain imperial authority against rebellious German princes and to preserve the empire's territorial integrity. Theophanu's Byzantine background influences her approach to governance and her diplomatic relations with the Byzantine Empire.
events.certainty_note: Theophanu's regency is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary sources. Her role as regent is certain.

events.title: Basil II Continues Balkan Campaigns
events.description: Basil II continues his military campaigns in the Balkans, consolidating Byzantine control over Bulgaria and neighboring regions. The campaigns are part of Basil's long-term strategy to restore Byzantine hegemony in the eastern Mediterranean and Balkans. In 983, Basil faces resistance from Samuel of Bulgaria and other local rulers, but Byzantine military superiority gradually prevails. The campaigns are costly in terms of resources and manpower, but they represent a major phase of Byzantine territorial expansion.
events.certainty_note: Basil II's Balkan campaigns are well-attested in Byzantine sources, but specific events for 983 are not always clearly delineated. The general characterization of 983 as part of the broader campaign period is probable.

events.title: Fatimid-Byzantine Tensions in the Mediterranean
events.description: Tensions between the Byzantine Empire and the Fatimid Caliphate continue in the Mediterranean, with both powers competing for naval supremacy and control of trade routes. The Fatimid navy, under the command of capable admirals, remains a formidable force, while the Byzantine navy is also strong. Raids and counter-raids occur along the coasts of the Levant, North Africa, and the Aegean. These naval conflicts are part of the broader geopolitical competition between the two major powers of the eastern Mediterranean in the late 10th century.
events.certainty_note: Byzantine-Fatimid naval conflicts are attested in sources, but specific events for 983 are not always clearly documented. The general characterization of ongoing tensions is probable.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact extent of Basil II's military success in the Balkans in 983 is not clearly documented in surviving sources. Some scholars argue that the Byzantine sources exaggerate Basil's achievements and that the conquest of Bulgaria was more gradual and contested than traditional narratives suggest. Additionally, the regency of Theophanu is sometimes portrayed in later sources as more stable than it actually was; contemporary evidence suggests significant internal conflict and instability within the Holy Roman Empire during this period.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 983 is dominated by Byzantine, Islamic, and Western European sources, reflecting the documentary bias toward literate, centralized states. The regency crisis in the Holy Roman Empire is well-documented in German chronicles, but these sources often reflect the perspectives of the imperial court and may exaggerate the stability or legitimacy of the regency. Eastern European and Scandinavian history relies on later compilations. Sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, and the Americas have no contemporary written records for this year.

graph_edges.note: Otto II's death leads to Theophanu's regency for Otto III
Theophanu's regency ends with Otto III's death in 1002
Basil II's 983 campaigns are part of the broader Balkan conquest
